coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots assisted photovoltage and efficiency enhancement in CdSe quantum dot sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Yuanyuan
Zhang, Hua
Pan, Dengyu
Wang, Liang
Zhong, Xinhua
coredata.dc:description: CdSe quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSCs) modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been successfully achieved in this work for the first time. Satisfactorily, the optimized photovoltage (V oc) of the modified QDSCs was approximately 0.04 V higher than that of plain CdSe QDSCs, consequently improving the photovoltaic performance of the resulting QDSCs. Served as a novel coating on the CdSe QD sensitized photoanode, GQDs played a vital role in improving V oc due to the suppressed charge recombination which has been confirmed by electron impedance spectroscopy as well as transient photovoltage decay measurements. Moreover, different adsorption sequences, concentration and deposition time of GQDs have also been systematically investigated to boost the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of CdSe QDSCs. After the coating of CdSe with GQDs, the resulting champion CdSe QDSCs exhibited an improved PCE of 6.59% under AM 1.5G full one sun illumination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
CdSe QDSCs
Coating
Open-circuit voltage
Power conversion efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MPA-capped CdSe QDs and GQDs
Photovoltaic performance of CdSe cells and CdSe-GQDs cells
Photovoltaic performance with different adsorption sequence of GQDs
Photovoltaic performance with different concentrations of GQDs
Photovoltaic performance with different deposition time of GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) J–V curves of CdSe (black) and CdSe-GQDs champion cells (red) and (b) the corresponding IPCE curves. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) J–V and (b) IPCE curves of QDSCs based on the different coating sequences of GQDs layer.

(a) J–V and (b) IPCE curves of champion CdSe QDSCs coated with different GQDs concentrations. The dependence of average photovoltaic performance based on different concentrations of GQDs: (c) dependence of V oc and J sc, (d) dependence of FF and PCE on GQDs concentration.
(a) J–V and (b) IPCE curves of CdSe QDSCs based on different deposition times of GQDs. The dependence of (c) V oc and J sc, (d) FF and PCE on the deposition times of GQDs.

EIS characterization of the CdSe–GQDs cells (red) compared with initial CdSe cells (black). (a) Chemical capacitance Cμ , (b) recombination resistance R rec, (c) dark current on corresponding applied voltage V app, (d) Nyquist plots of both cells at the forward bias of –0.60 V. Inset: equivalent circuit adopted for fitting EIS. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) V oc decay plots based on CdSe (black) and CdSe-GQDs cells (red), (b) electron lifetime derived from OCVD measurements. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Average photovoltaic parameters of CdSe and CdSe-GQDs cells.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The numbers in parentheses refer to the values obtained from the champion cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Average photovoltaic parameters of QDSCs based on the different coating sequence of GQDs layer.
Average photovoltaic parameters of QDSCs based on different deposition concentration of the GQDs solution.
Average photovoltaic parameters of QDSCs based on different adsorption times of GQDs solution.
Extracted values of resistance (R) and capacitance (C) of CdSe and CdSe-GQDs cells under the forward bias of −0.60 V.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots assisted photovoltage and efficiency enhancement in CdSe quantum dot sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, Institute of Applied Chemistry, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry
Institute of Applied Chemistry, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CdSe quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSCs) modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been successfully achieved in this work for the first time. Satisfactorily, the optimized photovoltage (V oc) of the modified QDSCs was approximately 0.04 V higher than that of plain CdSe QDSCs, consequently improving the photovoltaic performance of the resulting QDSCs. Served as a novel coating on the CdSe QD sensitized photoanode, GQDs played a vital role in improving V oc due to the suppressed charge recombination which has been confirmed by electron impedance spectroscopy as well as transient photovoltage decay measurements. Moreover, different adsorption sequences, concentration and deposition time of GQDs have also been systematically investigated to boost the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of CdSe QDSCs. After the coating of CdSe with GQDs, the resulting champion CdSe QDSCs exhibited an improved PCE of 6.59% under AM 1.5G full one sun illumination.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were coated on CdSe to fabricate CdSe-GQDs QDSCs and the improved PCE of 6.59% was achieved. GQD coating effectively suppressed charge recombination.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays people are more concerned with the protection of environment and the development of new energy for the sake of energy shortage and environmental deterioration. Emerged as one of the most potential candidates for the third-generation solar cells, quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSCs) have attracted increased attention of researchers [1–7]. According to the Shockley–Queisser limit, the theoretical value of the conversion efficiency of QDSCs could achieve as high as 33% [8,9]. Nevertheless, the current power conversion efficiency (PCE) of QDSCs lag remarkably behind that of theoretical value so far [10–13] although the reported latest efficiency has already reached the record of 8.2% [14]. The low PCE is associated with fill factor (FF), short-circuit current (J sc) and open-circuit potential (V oc). Commonly, copper sulfide has been employed as counter electrode to improve stability and PCE in QDSCs [15–19]. Besides, the enhancement of J sc has also been obtained primarily by expanding the light harvesting region [20–23] or increasing the amount of QDs loaded on TiO2 film [10–12,24]. Consequently, great improvement of V oc is urgent and challenged to boost the efficiency of the QDSCs. Besides exploitation of suitable redox shuttle, hindering charge recombination is another important approach that can improve V oc and the consequent PCE of cells [25]. To control charge recombination in sensitized solar cells, some reported wide band gap materials such as TiO2 [26], SiO2 [27], MgO [28], Al2O3 [29] and ZnS [30–33] have been exploited as a barrier layer on TiO2 photoanode to keep the photo-generated electrons from recombining the holes dwelling in the electrolyte [30–37]. Moreover, it is interesting to note that further reduction in charge recombination could be obtained with multilayer coating in comparison with a single layer. For instance, re-coating some molecular dipoles or SiO2 onto ZnS layer with resultant double barrier passivation has been reported to improve the performance of QDSCs [14,38,39].

For the past few years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) considered as a new class of novel, harmless quantum dots with fascinating and distinct properties have attracted extensive concern of researchers increasingly. Compared with both semiconductor quantum dots and graphene, GQDs exhibited more intense quantum confinement effect and boundary effect for its small size. Since its special physical and chemical properties including excellent fluorescence property, good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and chemical inertness, GQDs have been applied successfully in a multitude of exciting areas, ranging from photoelectric devices to sensors and biological imaging [40,41]. In addition, GQDs have also been widely used in organic photovoltaic, perovskite cells and dye sensitized solar cells (DSSC) to improve the photovoltaic performance [42–47]. Li et al. has ever utilized GQDs as sensitizers in DSSC but obtained the low PCE (<0.1%) for the low short-circuit current density (J sc of 200 µA/cm2) [48]. However, studies involved in the application of GQDs in QDSCs have not been reported so far.

In this work, we have successfully applied GQDs in QDSCs for the first time. It demonstrates that coating GQDs on CdSe QDs could effectively increase V oc and J sc because of the suppressed charge recombination in QDSCs. Different conditions of GQDs coating, including deposition sequence, concentration and time have been studied and optimized. In addition, the hindered charge recombination in CdSe-GQDs cell has been confirmed by electron impedance spectroscopy and transient photovoltage decay measurements. Upon passivation with GQDs layer, the resultant champion CdSe-GQDs QDSCs exhibited a maximum PCE of 6.59% under AM 1.5G full one sun illumination.

Cadmium oxide (CdO, 99.99%), selenium powder (200 mesh, 99.99%), 1-octadecene (ODE, 90%), trioctylphosphine (TOP, 90%) and oleylamine (OAm, 95%) were purchased from Aldrich. Oleic acid (90%), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 97%) were received from Alfa Aesar, and GQDs (about 3 nm) were obtained according to the literature [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MPA-capped CdSe QDs and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Cd precursor solution (0.4 M) was prepared by mixing 0.256 g CdO with 2.5 mL ODE and 2.5 mL oleic acid in a 50 mL flask, followed by evacuating under 90 °C. Then the temperature was set to 250 °C to dissolve the CdO powder. Finally, bright yellow Cd stock solution was obtained. The colorless Se precursor solution (1 M) was prepared by dissolving the 0.042 g Se powder in 0.5 mL TOP through ultrasonication. The organic phase high temperature route was applied to synthesize the initial OAm-capped oil-soluble CdSe QDs based on a literature method with a few minor changes [50]. Typically, 0.2 mL Se precursor solution was mixed with 4.5 mL OAm and 0.3 mL TOP in a flask, followed by evacuation under 90 oC and the solution was heated to 280 °C under N2 flow. Then, 0.5 mL Cd precursor solution was injected into the solution and maintained at this temperature for about 5 min. During the reaction, aliquots of the sample were taken at different time intervals to monitor the growth of CdSe QDs by recording UV–vis and PL spectra. Finally, the heating was ceased and the reaction mixture was cooled to 80 oC with further precipitation and centrifugation by using methanol and toluene. Final particle size of the oil-soluble OAm-capped CdSe QDs is about 5.5 nm. A ligand exchange process was utilized to make the initial oil-soluble QDs water-soluble through a bifunctional capping ligand MPA, referring to a specific literature [13,51,52]. The MPA-capped GQDs was also obtained via the ligand exchange process similar to the as mentioned above. Briefly, the prepared phase transfer agent solution like that in Refs [13,51,52] was added dropwise into the purified dodecylamine-capped GQDs in toluene with vigorous stirring for about 2 h. Eventually, the MPA-capped GQDs were collected from bottom and stored in dark condition.

CdSe-GQDs photoanodes were typically assembled as follows: First, 40 µL MPA-capped CdSe QDs were pipetted onto TiO2 mesoporous film and stayed for 3 h. Then the CdSe QDs sensitized photoanodes were rinsed sequentially with water and ethanol and dried with blower. Finally, the MPA-capped GQDs were pipetted onto the CdSe-sensitized TiO2 mesoporous film and absorbed for 1 h to get the best QDSCs performance, followed by rising with water and ethanol sequentially and dried with air. The prepared CdSe-GQDs photoanodes were alternately dipped into 0 1 M Zn(OAc)2 and 0.1 M Na2S aqueous solutions for 4 cycles along with 1 min each dip to deposit ZnS layer. After that, the ZnS coated photoanodes were dipped in 0.01 M tetraethylorthosilicate ethanol solution for 1 h to deposit SiO2 layer according to the method by Zhong et al. and then rinsed with water and ethanol and dried with blower [15].

The CdSe or CdSe-GQDs QDSCs were constructed by assembling the CdSe or CdSe-GQDs sensitized photoanode and Cu2S/brass counter electrode with a binder clip separated by a 60 µm thickness Scotch spacer. Polysulfide electrolyte solution composed of 2.0 M Na2S, 2.0 M S powder and 0.2 M KCl was then filled inside the cell device. In order to have reliable experimental results, at least five parallel QDSCs were assembled and measured for the average value of cells.

The current-voltage (J–V) characteristics of the QDSCs were measured by a Keithley 2400 source meter equipped under an AM 1.5G solar simulator (Oriel, Model no. 94022A equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp) and provided with simulated illumination. A NREL standard Si solar cell was called for calibrating intensity of the illumination at 100 mW/cm2 when testing. The available area of the device was 0.237 cm2 defined with shading mask. The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was obtained on a Keithley 2000 multimeter under the illumination of a 150 W tungsten lamp with a monochromator (Spectral Product DK 240). Both the open circuit voltage decay (OCVD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were actualized on an electrochemical workstation (Zennium, Zahner). OCVD was measured using a white LED with intensity of 100 mW/cm2 to illuminate the devices and then the light was switched, finally the dependence of photo-voltage would be recorded successively. In EIS measurements, device was measured by an impedance analyzer under dark condition and different forward bias ranged from 0 to 0.65 V were employed, adopting a 20 mV AC sinusoid during the constant applied bias with the frequency from 0.1 to 100 kHz in the meantime.

At present, high quality GQDs are commonly obtained by hydrothermal method, solvothermal treatment, chemical stripping carbon fiber, and the surface functional groups of the as-prepared GQDs are usually –OH, –C=O [41,53,54]. Herein, MPA-capped water-soluble GQDs were obtained by our developed ligand exchange process. As is known, MPA is one of the most common bifunctional linker molecules containing both carboxyl and thiol at the terminal respectively. The dodecylamine of the initial oil-soluble GQDs was replaced by the carboxyl of the MPA in the ligand exchange process, and then the other terminal group thiol could directly bind with CdSe sensitized photoanode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photovoltaic performance of CdSe cells and CdSe-GQDs cells
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the cell performance of CdSe and CdSe-GQDs cells (the concentration of GQDs solution was 0.5 g/L), different batches of the cells were fabricated and characterized. The J–V and IPCE curves from the champion cells of the two types are shown in Fig. 1(a) and 1(b), respectively. The average photovoltaic parameters of the corresponding QDSCs from J-V curves are listed in Table 1
. From both Fig. 1(a) and Table 1, it is obvious that the value of V oc increased significantly from the initial 0.606 V to 0.646 V (about 0.04 V higher than that of CdSe cells). Besides, as presented in Fig. 1(a) and Table 1, QDSCs with GQDs coating show higher J sc than CdSe cells, which is also reflected from the IPCE in Fig. 1(b). Apparently, the higher V oc and J sc are contributed to the addition of GQDs, which is considered as a thin energy barrier to reduce charge recombination as further discussed in the following EIS and OCVD analysis. However, together with the changes of the other parameters, the final obtained average PCEs for CdSe and CdSe-GQDs cells are 6.10% and 6.53%, respectively, with the maximum PCE of 6.59% for the latter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photovoltaic performance with different adsorption sequence of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth noting that the adsorption sequence of GQDs layer is extremely important to the properties of the resulting cell devices. When GQDs were first deposited followed by the deposition of CdSe, the assembled cells are referred to as GQDs–CdSe cells (the concentration of GQDs solution was 0.5 g/L). The results from the two deposition sequence are shown in Fig. 2 and Table 2 . It is quite clear that the photovoltaic performance of GQDs–CdSe cell is poorer than that of CdSe–GQDs cell because of the lower values of V oc and J sc. The difference can be ascribed to that GQDs coating can suppress the charge recombination more efficiently for CdSe–GQDs cell instead of GQDs–CdSe cell. Furthermore, the performance of GQDs-CdSe cell is also poor in comparison with the plain CdSe cell, because the GQDs deposited on the mesoporous TiO2 film will decrease the loaded amount of CdSe QDs to a certain extent, leading to a slight decrease of the J sc (from 15.25 mA/cm2 to 15.11 mA/cm2). Basing on the results that the performance of the CdSe–GQDs cell device is superior to the initial CdSe and GQDs-CdSe cell devices, we carried out the other experiments described in this work by using the technique of coating CdSe QDs with GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photovoltaic performance with different concentrations of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the influence of GQDs concentration on the performance of cells and determine the optimal concentration of GQDs employed, GQDs solution with different concentrations were deposited on CdSe sensitized photoanodes to assemble QDSCs. The obtained J-V and IPCE curves are presented in Fig. 3(a) and 3(b) and specific photovoltaic parameters are listed in Table 3.

As can be seen from Table 3, the value of V oc increased significantly with the increase of the GQDs concentration. From the other two parameters, J sc and FF, we can see that a slight increase occurred to J sc but an obvious decline for the FF. However, since the influence of the noticeable increases in V oc and J sc outperformed the decrease of FF, PCE values improved and reached the optimum results of 6.51% when the concentration of the GQDs solution was 0.5 g/L. The variation tendency of the photovoltaic parameters is shown in Fig. 3(c) and 3(d). In this scenario, we can clearly see that only a certain concentration of GQDs solution (∼0.5 g/L) would result in noticeable enhancement of PCE, while higher or lower concentration would lead to the unsatisfied results, especially the decrease in FF. Research has revealed that the coating shell with an optimized thickness, such as ZnS [33–36], SiO2 [30], TiO2 [29], could suppress the charge recombination occurring at the QDs surface, because of the band alignment of the coating materials and the QD sensitizers. In this work, GQDs own a wider bandgap compared with CdSe QDs, so we consider the GQDs coating could also act as energy barrier to prevent charge recombination and enhance V oc and J sc. In addition, the thickness of the coating shell is of great importance, because excessive coating of GQDs would deteriorate the performance of CdSe QD sensitizers in terms of hole transformation, also illustrating the fall of the parameter FF. Combining all the impacts of the GQDs coating, it is worth noting that introducing GQDs to QDSCs could indeed provide better performance of the cells with an optimized deposition concentration of the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photovoltaic performance with different deposition time of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Besides the influence of concentration of GQDs solution, the impact of the deposition time of GQDs has also been investigated at 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 h, respectively. The J–V and IPCE curves as well as average photovoltaic parameters of QDSCs are shown in Fig. 4
and Table 4 (the deposition concentration of GQDs solution are ∼0.5 g/L). As shown in Fig. 4(a) and Table 4, V oc and J sc are improved with the increase of the deposition time of GQDs, similar to the results caused by GQDs concentration. Oppositely, the photovoltaic parameter FF decreases distinctly because excessively thick GQDs barrier coating can suppress electrons and holes transfer. Based on the comprehensive effect of the three photovoltaic parameters, the PCE increase within 1 h and decrease at longer deposition time, during which the optimal PCE value reached 6.53% (V oc = 0.646 V, J sc = 15.73 mA/cm2, FF = 63.96%) when the deposition time was 1 h. The dependence of average photovoltaic performance based on the different deposition times of GQDs are shown in Fig. 4(c) and 4(d). The improvement in PCE of CdSe–GQDs cell could be contributed by the suppression of charge recombination with an optimized thickness of GQDs barrier layer.

To further illustrate the mechanism of GQDs coating which could improve the cell performance, EIS spectra recorded from CdSe and CdSe-GQDs cells prepared at optimized coating conditions were studied to research charge transport and recombination. As shown in Fig. 5(a) and Table 5 , no significant changes in chemical capacitance Cμ could be observed for the two cells, suggesting that both of the two typed cells have invariant TiO2 conduction band and electron density. Even so, the value of recombination resistance (R rec) for CdSe–GQDs exceeds those of CdSe cell at different V app as shown in Fig. 5(b). From this, it can be concluded that GQDs as the coating layer in CdSe–GQDs cell is positive to suppress charge recombination in the interface of QDs and electrolyte. Instead, the dark current presented in Fig. 5(c) for CdSe–GQDs cell is lower compared with CdSe cell, also implying the less back electrons contributed by GQDs coating as a barrier to restrain the electron recombination. In order to compare more intuitively, Nyquist plots of CdSe and CdSe–GQDs cells at the forward bias of –0.60 V are shown in Fig. 5(d). It could be observed that the semi arc for CdSe–GQDs cell is larger than that of CdSe cell, indicative of the larger charge transfer resistance between photoanode and electrolyte in CdSe–GQDs cell.

Open circuit voltage decay (OCVD) measurements were also performed to research the electron recombination process as illustrated in Fig. 6. During the test process, a white LED with light intensity of 100 mW/cm2 was employed to apply stable voltage on cells in the beginning, and then the open circuit voltage decay information was recorded as the light turned off. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the potentials for the two typed cells maintain steady at their open circuit voltages under illumination, and then keep on decaying to almost 0 V in dark. Nevertheless the two cells have a dramatic distinction in decay time. Obviously, the decay time of CdSe–GQDs cell is longer than that of the plain CdSe cell, demonstrating that the charge recombination rate dramatically reduces in the existence of GQDs coating layer. Furthermore, corresponding to V oc decay rate plot, the figure of electron lifetime (τn ) is represented in Fig. 6(b). It is very clear that the electron lifetime of the CdSe–GQDs cell is longer than that of CdSe cell at the corresponding V oc, indicating that the GQDs coating is beneficial to the photovoltaic performance of the resulting cells in comparison with CdSe cells.

In conclusion, with the assistance of GQDs deposition on QDs, the efficiency for CdSe QDSC is enhanced strongly contributed by the improved V oc and J sc. Different conditions of GQDs deposition, including concentration, time and sequence have been investigated to optimize the PCE of cells. A champion PCE of 6.59% has been achieved under the optimized condition. Here, GQDs serve as a novel surface coating layer that can reduce charge recombination occurring at the QDs/electrolyte interface. The suppressed charge recombination in CdSe–GQDs cell has been confirmed by EIS and OCVD. In addition, experimental results also show that the GQDs deposition on TiO2 film before CdSe loading will not be favorable to the cell performance, due to the decrease in current density. It is meaningful to employ the eco-friendly and abundant materials of GQDs in QDSCs. Basing on the improved PCE, V oc and J sc, more exciting breakthroughs for QDSCs with the assistance of carbon materials will be achieved in the near future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21175043, 91233102) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities for financial support.
